petitioner_background: The petitioner is a researcher and assistant professor in the Department of Radiation Oncology at a university, focusing his work in radiation biology, neuroscience, and Gulf War Illness (GWI). He received his Ph.D. in pharmaceutical sciences in 2008 and completed initial postdoctoral training at another university before joining his current institution. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under INA § 203(b)(1)(A) (EB-1A) and intends to continue his research in radiation biology, neuroscience, and GWI.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted several media articles published on the websites of organizations and publications such as Science Daily, NBC News, The Scientist Magazine, The Economic Times, NDTV and The Hindu. These articles reported on scholarly articles written by the petitioner and his colleagues about the effects of radiation on the brain and central nervous system, due to either space travel or cancer treatment. Several of these articles identified the petitioner as a researcher who contributed to the research results discussed, and some included the same quote from him. Several of the publications carried a Reuters article with little or no modification.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO disagreed with the Director's finding that this criterion was met and withdrew that portion of the Director's decision. While several articles identified the petitioner as a researcher who contributed to the research, none of the articles were about him — they were instead about the research and its conclusions and implications. Citing Negro-Plumpe v. Okin, the AAO noted that articles that are not about the petitioner do not meet this regulatory criterion (analogous to articles about a show not being about the actor).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence established that the petitioner served as a peer reviewer for articles submitted to journals such as Radiation Research, Food, and Cancer Biotherapy and Radiopharmaceuticals. In addition, he serves as an editor for the journals Innovare Journal of Health Science and Indo Global Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director's finding that the petitioner met this criterion based on his peer review work for multiple scientific journals and his editorial roles.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted ten reference letters claiming original scientific contributions of major significance in radiation biology, neuroscience, and GWI treatment. Letters came from collaborators including one from the University who supervised the petitioner for approximately eight years, another collaborator who described four research projects including development of a computer model to assess effects of radiation on the brain, a professor from another university familiar with the petitioner's work on effects of low dose radiation on neural stem and precursor cells (citing his article in Antioxidants and Redox Signaling), and a letter discussing his GWI research exposing rats to low doses of chemicals to which Gulf War veterans had been exposed. The petitioner also submitted: The Gulf War Illness Landscape document from the Department of Defense Gulf War Illness Research Program; Altmetric Attention Score data for six of his papers including one co-authored article published in Scientific Reports; Clarivate Analytics tables showing 'baseline' citation rates in broad fields for 2008-2018 with percentiles; data showing a paper in Experimental Neurology was cited 14 times (claimed 16.66 times higher than average); data showing some papers placed in top 10% or top 1% for 'neuroscience and behavior'; and review articles citing his work, including one published in Nature Reviews Neurology citing three of his articles (one defining the term 'early' and another identifying brain structures most commonly affected by radiation treatment).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. Several reference letters shared a common structure with titled sections devoted to regulatory criteria and included frequent repetition of regulatory language (e.g., headings stating the petitioner 'has made innovative and original scientific contributions of major significance'). The AAO cited Surinder Singh v. BIA and Mei Chai Ye v. U.S. Dept. of Justice, noting that strikingly similar language in affidavits allows the trier of fact to infer the petitioner is the source of the content, greatly diminishing evidentiary value. Letters also used phrases like research 'been reduced to' papers, which appeared in the petitioner's own written statement. Letters described outside second or third-hand information (media circulation, citation data) that appeared in separate evidence, further diminishing value. The supervising collaborator did not provide insights into the petitioner's specific role despite eight years of collaboration, stating only that he 'has had an impact on my productivity in research.' Letters relied on journal prestige and popular media attention, but the AAO would not assume all papers in prestigious journals have impact, and popular media attention does not reflect significant contribution to the scientific field. Regarding the RAC claim that the petitioner's work was 'frequently referenced,' the record lacked a RAC report, and The Gulf War Illness Landscape contained only a single citation. Total citation counts may be relevant in final merits but don't demonstrate major significance of individual contributions. The Altmetric Attention Score measures social media and mainstream news attention, which doesn't demonstrate major significance to other scientists. Clarivate Analytics citation comparisons — a paper cited 14 times vs. average, or 9 vs. 3 citations — don't show the research made a contribution of noticeably greater significance. The AAO noted it was not imposing an 'exclusivity' requirement but evaluating whether work was highlighted; the Nature Reviews Neurology review cited the petitioner's articles as one of three sources for certain definitions/findings, which indicated reliance but did not highlight his work as particularly noteworthy or impactful. Repetition of regulatory language does not satisfy the burden of proof (citing Fedin Bros. and Avyr Associates).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that he has coauthored more than 40 articles published in scientific journals.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that the petitioner met this criterion. The AAO withdrew the Director's finding of a discrepancy between the number of articles claimed and those evidenced, noting the record includes sufficient documentation of the petitioner's articles.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that he was named editor for the United States for Indo Global Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences (IGJPS) in 2018, with a letter from the editor-in-chief describing his role reviewing original papers, providing guidelines to authors, establishing policies on conflict of interest for authors, reviewers and editors. The editor-in-chief described IGJPS as having been in publication for more than seven years with a Google Scholar 'h-index' of 19 and 'i10 index' of 44, and information from the journal's website describing its focus in pharmacy and pharmaceutical sciences. For Innovare Journal of Health Science (IJHS), the petitioner provided a page from the journal's website listing him on the editorial board (consisting of an editor-in-chief, associate editor, two assistant editors, and twelve editors), and a letter from the publisher describing his responsibilities for maintaining smooth flow of manuscripts, verifying compliance with the manual of style, assisting with author guidelines, assigning manuscripts to reviewers, and making acceptance decisions. He submitted pages from the publisher's website indicating IJHS is a peer-reviewed, bimonthly open access journal in publication since 2013, plus information about the publisher. The petitioner also submitted evidence of agreement to serve on a university Council, indicating he is one of several faculty members responsible to 'advise the Chancellor and the Division on issues in the areas of teaching, student life and welfare, and intramural and intercollegiate athletics.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. While the evidence established that the petitioner plays a leading role for both IGJPS and IJHS, he failed to establish that either journal has a distinguished reputation. For IGJPS, the letter did not explain the meaning of the h-index and i10 index values or provide context demonstrating the journal distinguished itself among other scientific publications; the website material simply described its focus. For IJHS, the website evidence did not include information showing a distinguished reputation among scientific journals. Regarding the university Council, the evidence did not demonstrate that the petitioner plays a leading or critical role for the Council (he is one of several faculty members) or that the council enjoys a distinguished reputation.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to submit required initial evidence meeting at least three of the ten regulatory criteria — he only met two (judging and scholarly articles). The AAO withdrew the Director's finding on published material and rejected the original contributions and leading role criteria. Because the initial evidentiary threshold was not met, the AAO stated it need not provide a final merits determination under Kazarian. Nevertheless, the AAO reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the petitioner established the required sustained national or international acclaim, nor that he is among the small percentage at the very top of his field, noting that even major league athletes do not automatically meet the extraordinary ability standard.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Surinder Singh v. Board of Immigration Appeals, 438 F.3d 145 (2d Cir. 2006)
Mei Chai Ye v. U.S. Dept. of Justice, 489 F.3d 517 (2d Cir. 2007)
Fedin Bros. Co., Ltd. v. Sava, 724 F. Supp. 1103 (E.D.N.Y. 1989), aff'd, 905 F.2d 41 (2d Cir. 1990)
Avyr Associates, Inc. v. Meissner, No. 95 CIV. 10729 (S.D.N.Y. Apr. 18, 1997)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO declined to consider new evidence submitted with the appeal, citing Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena, because the Director had issued an RFE explaining deficiencies and the petitioner had been given an opportunity to respond. The AAO also raised concerns about the authenticity/independence of reference letters: several letters shared strikingly similar structure with titled sections repeating regulatory language, and contained phrases (like research being 'reduced to' papers) that also appeared in the petitioner's own written statement, leading the AAO to infer that the petitioner or his representative was the actual source of the content, greatly diminishing the letters' evidentiary value. Letters also contained second/third-hand information about media outlets and citation data that appeared in separate record evidence, further indicating the source of the content.
